indexedPages.subject: Language Arts
essentialOutcomes: The learner will explore, use, and critically apply oral language for pleasure, personal growth, to form and foster relationships and, to develop an appreciation of and celebration of culture and of oral languages.
specificOutcomes: 1.1 Choose to listen to music, poetry and stories for pleasure.
1.2 Connect environmental sounds to meaning.
1.3 Use different voices in role playing to indicate tone and mood.
1.4 Describe how musical and environmental sounds affect mood.
1.5 Use Listening Comprehension Strategies to make and simplify meaning: ask questions, recall ideas, predict, visualise, and make connections.
1.6 Listen to, retell, and express an opinion about the story.
1.7 Listen to differentiate between make-believe and truth.
1.8 Listen to follow and give direction e.g. 2-3 steps.
1.9 Listen and respond with increasing understanding and confidence to conversations expressed in Standard English.
1.10 Use turn taking strategies as a listener and a speaker.
1.11 Identify and use some non-verbal cues (e.g., facial expressions, gestures etc.) and in oral communication with thoughtfulness.
1.12 Listen, share information ask and answer questions on a topic opinion in response to views articulated by others.
1.13 Use and respond to Home Language(s) with awareness and purpose to greet, thank, make a request, explain, apologise and issue an invitation.
1.14 Continue to develop Home Language and Standard English vocabulary, phrasing and sentence use to share ideas.
1.15 Describe sounds, images, events, people, and places with clarity that includes: relevant details and a range of descriptive words reflecting size, colour, intensity, etc.
1.16 Produce complete sentences when suitable to task and setting.
1.17 Continue to develop the foundation of phonological awareness by orally identifying, producing, and manipulating various units of speech sounds within words.
1.18 Use common singular and plural nouns in the meaningful context of sentences.
1.19 Begin to use common irregular plural forms, such as man/men, child/children, and foot/feet.
1.20 Use common pronouns with increasing understanding (I, me, mine, they, them, theirs, him, his, hers).

eloGroups.elo: ELO 1: The learner will explore, use, and critically apply oral language for pleasure, personal growth, to form and foster relationships and, to develop an appreciation of and celebration of culture and of oral languages.

indexedPages.subject: Language Arts
essentialOutcomes: ELO 2: The learner will demonstrate a variety of ways to use background knowledge and interests to select and engage critically with a range of culturally diverse paper-based, visual, and digital texts for pleasure and personal growth.
ELO 3: The learner will interact with understanding and critical thought to a variety of genres and text forms using comprehension strategies, vocabulary, language structures, and graphophonic cues.

ELO 4: Students will develop their understanding of how an author's purpose and style, genre, text form, text features and choice of vocabulary, language influence the meaning of text and define the author's craft.
specificOutcomes: 2.1 Reflect on and connect interests and background knowledge before, during and after reading.
2.2 Apply Concepts About Print to navigate Emergent/Early level text.
2.3 Connect background knowledge to new learning.
2.4 Regard reading/viewing as sources of interest, enjoyment, and information.
2.5 Engage in reading or reading-like behaviour to experience a variety of text and text forms.
2.6 Participate in opportunities to share favourite texts with peers.
2.7 Re-read, retell and act out selections of familiar stories, poems, and nonfiction texts.
2.8 Select, with teacher assistance, texts appropriate to their interests and reading progress.
2.9 Select just right texts with assistance and beginning independence.
2.10 Use illustrations in a text to describe and interpret characters, setting, and events.
2.11 Use nonfiction instructions and text features to create models or artistic representations.
2.12 Formulate questions as well as understandings of digital fiction and nonfiction text.
2.13 Develop an understanding and respect for diversity in text.
3.1 Share and discuss texts with peers.
3.2 Use illustrations, graphics etc. to ask and answer questions, draw conclusions and make inferences, identify details, synthesise main idea from the details.
3.3 Retell the beginning, middle, and end of stories and events using images, drama, models and written summaries.
3.4 Use a variety of comprehension strategies to create meaning in poetry, fiction, and nonfiction texts during Independent Reading.
3.5 Demonstrate understanding through various responses to text: artistic representations, discussions, graphic organisers, oral and written responses to questions.
3.6 Continue to make connections between Home Language vocabulary and Standard English vocabulary.
3.7 Apply known oral vocabulary within familiar language patterns to make meaning from text.
3.8 Continue to develop use of context clues to solve unknown vocabulary.
3.9 Begin to automatically read and understand sight and high-frequency words in a variety of reading contexts.
3.10 Demonstrate understanding and application of synonyms, antonyms, and homophones.
3.11 Begin to understand how Emergent/Early level prefixes and suffixes change the meaning of words.
3.12 Expand vocabulary by noticing and using frequently occurring root words to read inflectional forms.
3.13 Develop visualisation strategies to connect words and phrases to meaningful mental images.
3.14 Demonstrate understanding of how word order in fiction and nonfiction text impacts meaning.
3.15 Begin to apply punctuation and bold print to guide intonation and expression.
3.16 Apply automatic knowledge of known sight words within sentences to develop phrasing, fluency, and expression.
3.17 Continue to recognise and meaningfully apply Emergent/Early stage knowledge of letter patterns to decode unfamiliar words.
3.18 Apply knowledge of word order, illustrations, text content and letter sounds to identify unknown words.
3.19 Begin to monitor for errors by cross checking phonics, meaning, and structure clues.
4.1 Begin to become familiar with the style of local and favourite international authors.
4.2 Engage with and participate in Shared Reading and Readers Theatre in fiction, nonfiction, and poetry.
4.3 Develop an understanding of text features such as bold print and font size adds to an author's style.
4.4 Ask questions of author's purpose for writing.
4.5 Continue to develop understanding and purpose of fiction, nonfiction, and poetry genres.
4.6 Apply some text features to locate and predict information in text.
4.7 Read and follow simple written and/or illustrated instructions.
4.8 Use text features to support individual and/or small group research on topic of interest.
4.9 Search for answers to questions on digital sites.
4.10 Discuss interesting words that evoke emotions.
4.11 Create a personal word bank.
4.12 Develop an understanding that words may have similar meanings.
4.13 Continue to develop an understanding of how written word order impacts meaning.
4.14 Compare texts by same illustrator for similarities in mood.
4.15 Explore how emoticons, symbols, and shapes are used to convey or enhance meaning.

eloGroups.elo: ELO 2: The learner will demonstrate a variety of ways to use background knowledge and interests to select and engage critically with a range of culturally diverse paper-based, visual, and digital texts for pleasure and personal growth.
ELO 3: The learner will interact with understanding and critical thought to a variety of genres and text forms using comprehension strategies, vocabulary, language structures, and graphophonic cues.

ELO 4: Students will develop their understanding of how an author's purpose and style, genre, text form, text features and choice of vocabulary, language influence the meaning of text and define the author's craft.

indexedPages.subject: Language Arts
essentialOutcomes: ELO 5: Learners will generate, gather and organize thoughts to explore, clarify and reflect on ideas, feelings and experiences as they create a written or representative draft, independently and collaboratively, for a range of audiences and purposes.

ELO 6: Learners will revise the organization, and language use (vocabulary and grammar) of drafted writing or representation, collaboratively and independently, for a variety of purposes and audiences.
ELO 7: Learners will use their knowledge of spoken language, written language and writing conventions to refine the precision and enhance the meaning and clarity of their written work.
specificOutcomes: 5.1 Begin to develop strategies for the prewriting stages of the writing process.
5.2 Talk about the ideas they plan to write about.
5.3 Draft some illustrations to accompany ideas and write thoughts that match the ideas in their drawings.
5.4 Use role play to assist with brainstorming ideas.
5.5 Begin to use simple graphic organizers to brainstorm and plan.
5.6 Begin to become familiar with the purpose of various genres and how different genres look and sound.
5.7 Begin to develop strategies for connecting prewriting to the drafting stages of the writing process.
5.8 Write to match text to their brainstorm, planning and drawings.
5.9 Begin to identify the topic, purpose, audience and form for writing.
5.10 Begin to experiment with creating a draft in different forms.
5.11 Begin to experiment with writing in simple sentences that may include descriptive words or simple transition words.
5.12 Begin to organise sentences into paragraphs by writing narratives with two or more appropriately sequenced events.
5.13 With assistance, begin to become familiar with the keyboard for individual or shared writing.
5.14 Work with a partner, in small groups and independently, to create a draft writing in both print and/or digital format.
5.15 Continue to use role plays to assist with drafting process planning, convey and enhance meaning.
5.16 Apply lessons learned about writing to record feelings, thoughts and ideas during Independent or Journal writing time.
6.1 Engage in teacher assisted and peer collaboration to review the organisation of the written draft through the process of ARMS: Add, Remove, Move, Substitute.
6.2 Revisit brainstorming and illustration to add details to and/or delete details.
6.3 Reread their draft and begin to recognize where they can make changes to better organise the beginning, middle, and end.
6.4 Experiment with developing writing with a sense of flow.
6.5 Begin to establish a personal voice in their writing by using pictures and words that convey their attitude or feelings.
6.6 Experiment with using multi-sensory words to improve writing.
6.7 Begin to use comparison words to distinguish one thing from another.
6.8 Monitor and revise writing to ensure simple but complete sentences are meaningful.
6.9 Develop more understanding of how and when to use descriptive or action words to extend meaning of draft.
6.10 Apply knowledge of spoken language to know when to use pronouns, plurals, and words indicating position.
6.11 Experiment with some variety in first sentences (leads) of writing.
6.12 Demonstrate understanding of the connection between word order and meaningful writing.
6.13 Begin to use various types of sentences: declarative, question, and exclamation.
6.14 Use techniques demonstrated by favorite authors.
6.15 Develop language use when revising written drafts — use expressive language and voice techniques as demonstrated by favourite authors.
6.16 Use expressive language to elaborate and create interest.
7.1 Develop increased confidence and accuracy to increase spelling and punctuation accuracy, tidiness and presentation.
7.2 Write from left to right and from top to bottom consistently.
7.3 Use some conventional spacing.
7.4 Continue to develop a relaxed hand while writing.
7.5 Attempt to make letter formation readable by various audiences.
7.6 Monitor and check high frequency words with the aid of environmental print, word walls, personal dictionaries.
7.7 Generate temporary and accurate spelling based on phonological strategies.
7.8 Continue to develop spelling of single syllable words by applying knowledge of consonants, blends, digraphs, and vowel sounds.
7.9 Become increasingly familiar with predictable letter patterns to spell more accurately.
7.10 Apply letter and word knowledge strategies during the writing process to monitor, edit errors and check for correctness.
7.11 Apply sight word knowledge gathered through reading and writing to try out different spellings.
7.12 Begin to be aware of digital features such as spell check.
7.13 Spell commonly used sight words and phonetically regular words with increasing correctness in final copies.
7.14 Begin to add a few grade-level punctuation conventions: full stops, question marks, exclamation marks, capitals.
7.15 Develop hand created or digital presentation styles using various forms of illustrations, titles, fonts.
7.16 Participate in opportunities to share a chosen piece of writing with peers or an invited audience.

essentialOutcomes: ELO 1: Use materials to design a solution to a human problem by mimicking how plants and/or animals use their external parts to help them survive, grow and meet their needs.
ELO 2: Read texts and use media to determine patterns in behaviour of parent and offspring that help offspring survive.
ELO 3: Make observations to construct an evidence-based account that young plants and animals are like, but not exactly like their parents.
specificOutcomes: K: Define the terms: Mimic/mimicking, Protection, Living things, Non-living things, Defence, Camouflage, Grow, Nutrients, Predators.
K: Name and describe some living and non-living things in the environment.
K: Identify and name some characteristics of living things.
K: Name and describe the external parts of local animals and plants and discuss their functions.
K: Identify and describe natural defences that local animals use to help them survive.
K: Describe mimicking in their own words.
K: Give examples of how humans mimic plants and/or animals to solve problems.
K: Identify some human problems that have been solved by mimicking how plants and/or animals use their external parts.
S: Classify some common things as either living or non-living.
S: Observe examples of how plants and/or animals use their external parts for survival.
S: Communicate properties of mimicked plants and animals by observing and describing.
S: Use simple materials to design a solution to a human problem using examples from plants and animals.
S: Construct a simple model of a gadget designed to solve the problem and test its effectiveness.
V: Appreciate that the external parts of plants and animals have inspired technological designs.
V: Demonstrate a continuing interest in the link between nature and technological design.
V: Willingly participate in classroom discussions and group activities.
V: Show a respect for living things while making observations.
V: Observe all applicable safety measures when interacting with specimens or using tools.
V: Display sensitivity and offer assistance to peers who may have challenges.
K: Define the terms: Protect, Signal, Offspring, Survive, Behaviour, Parents, Respond.
K: Name and identify the offspring of some common animals.
K: Identify signals offspring make (crying, chirping, whining, and other vocalisations).
K: Describe how different patterns in behaviour teach survival skills.
S: Observe how parents and offspring behave to help them to survive.
S: Demonstrate through role play the patterns of behaviour that offspring make and the parent's responses.
S: Infer from the offspring's signal, the parent's response.
S: Interpret sounds and pictures to make judgements about parent/offspring behaviours.
S: Communicate their observations of parent-offspring behaviours.
V: Appreciate that parents and offspring work together to survive.
V: Show respect for the parents and offspring by treating them with care.
V: While making observations students will show concern for safety.
V: Appreciate what human parents do for their children to make sure they thrive.
V: Display sensitivity and offer assistance to peers who may have challenges.
V: Participate actively in classroom discussions.
K: Define the terms: parent, roots, leaves, stems, fruits, flowers, body coverings.
K: Identify the properties of plants that allow you to distinguish between parents and offspring.
K: Identify the properties of animals that allow you to distinguish between parents and offspring.
S: Observe and compare how young animals and plants are like, but not exactly like their parents.
S: Collect and record evidence that young plants and animals are like, but not exactly like, their parents.
S: Compare and contrast parents and offspring who don't have the same features.
S: Work collaboratively with peers as you observe physical features of living things.
S: Communicate ideas with new vocabulary.
V: Respect for evidence arising in experiments and demonstrations.
V: Care and respect for living things.
V: Appreciation that young plants and animals are like but not exactly like their parents.
V: Display sensitivity and offer assistance to peers who may have challenges.
V: Participate actively in classroom discussions.
V: Work in the classroom with concerns for safety.

indexedPages.subject: Science
essentialOutcomes: ELO 1: Learners will be able to plan and conduct investigations to provide evidence that vibrating materials can make sound and that sound can make materials vibrate.
ELO 2: Make observations to construct an evidence-based account that objects can be seen only when illuminated.
ELO 3: Plan and conduct an investigation to determine the effect of placing objects made with different materials in the path of a beam of light.
ELO 4: Use tools and materials to design and build a device that uses light or sound to solve the problem of communicating over a distance.
specificOutcomes: K: Define the terms: Sounds, vibrate, waves, loud, soft, sign language.
K: Demonstrate an understanding of how to produce sounds.
K: Compare and describe sounds produced as loud, soft, pleasant, unpleasant.
K: Discuss and identify the effects and uses of sounds in our daily lives.
K: Show that sound is a wave - like water is a wave.
S: Classify sounds according to loudness.
S: Investigate how sounds are produced and its effects on objects.
S: Observe the effects of sounds on objects.
S: Predict the effects of sounds on objects and the sounds made by different materials.
S: Communicate effectively the results of sound experiments.
V: Work respectfully with others in exploring and investigating sounds.
V: Show respect for persons with hearing or speaking impairment.
V: Willingly observe, question, and explore the effects of sounds.
V: Show interest and curiosity about sounds and their uses.
V: Develop an awareness that loud sounds affect people and animals.
K: Define the terms: light/darkness, artificial, natural, reflect.
K: Give examples of the sources of light.
K: Identify different light sources in and out of the classroom.
K: Describe why objects can be seen when illuminated.
K: Understand that the sun is a natural source of light.
K: Understand that the spherical moon reflects the sun's light.
S: Classify light according to their source (natural, artificial).
S: Investigate how objects can be seen.
S: Observe the effects of light on objects.
S: Predict the effects of turning on and off the lights in a room.
S: Communicate ideas about light using new words.
S: Safe use of candles as a source of light.
V: Willingly participate in and contribute to classroom discussions.
V: Develop an awareness that light must be present for objects to be seen.
V: Appreciate the importance of light for warning and safety.
V: Show appreciation for and assist persons with visual impairment.
K: Define the terms: glass, transparent, translucent, reflective, opaque.
K: Demonstrate they understand that objects can block varying amounts of light from passing.
K: Account for the use of different types of glass for different applications.
K: Explain that only clear and smooth glass can allow most light to pass through.
K: Understand that rough glass is more likely to allow less light to pass through.
S: Observe the amount of light that is blocked by different objects.
S: Classify objects placed in the beam of light as transparent, translucent, reflective or opaque.
S: Predict which types of glass or objects may be best suited for different applications.
V: Appreciate that understanding how light interacts with objects may impact our use of objects.
V: Interest and curiosity to investigate light properties.
V: Inventiveness - how could we use our understanding to design useful products.
V: Display sensitivity and offer assistance to peers who may have challenges.
V: Participate actively in classroom discussions.
K: Define the terms: warning, communicating, siren, emergency vehicle, traffic lights.
K: Demonstrate they understand the concept of warning sounds.
K: Give examples of communicating with sound.
K: Give examples of communicating with light.
K: Recognize that communication in cities and rural areas may be different.
K: Recognize that communication technologies have varied over the last century.
S: Observe and identify light and sound communication in video accounts.
S: Infer certain messages from the type of sound heard.
S: Infer certain messages from the light patterns detected.
S: Construct a simple sound communication device (cup phones).
S: Formulate models of communication devices that use sound or light or both.
S: Practice new vocabulary in discussions.
V: Appreciate that different sounds and light have different meanings.
V: Show inventiveness in designing a communication device.
V: Collaborate to make models of communication devices.
V: Recognize that light and sound are used as safety and warning signals.
V: Demonstrate interest and curiosity regarding how communication devices have evolved.

indexedPages.subject: Social Studies
essentialOutcomes: To understand that we have roles and responsibilities in our families, school, and community
specificOutcomes: K: Understand the importance of keeping their home, school, and community tidy and clean.
S: Practice putting garbage and litter in trash cans at school and home.
V: Observe and appreciate places in their environment that are kept clean and tidy.
K: Recognize that when everyone in our family shares and helps one another we all benefit.
S: List the ways that our siblings, parents, grandparents, and other relatives help us and vice versa.
V: Appreciate that helping other family members is important for our collective wellbeing.
K: Identify persons to turn to when they need help.
S: Communicate ways to express when they feel unsafe.
V: Appreciate feeling safe in their environment.
K: Understand what a leader is.
S: Identify leaders in their home, school, church, community, and nation.
V: Appreciate that leaders are an important part of our well-being.
K: Recognize the appropriate manners and behaviours towards adults in positions of authority.
S: Practice using proper manners when interacting with adults.
V: Respect persons in authority.
indexedPages.summary: To understand that we have roles and responsibilities in our families, school, and community. Focusing Questions: What are our roles and responsibilities in our family and school? 1. Who is a leader?

essentialOutcomes: To be aware that we all have things we need and things we want
specificOutcomes: K: Identify basic human needs.
S: Distinguish between our basic needs and our wants.
V: Appreciate the difference between needs and wants.
K: Understand that we meet most of our needs and wants through goods and services.
S: Categorise our needs and wants as goods or services.
V: Appreciate that goods and services are necessary to meet our needs and wants.
K: Identify things in the environment that we use.
S: Discuss how we use things in the environment.
V: Appreciate that the environment can be used to provide our needs and wants.
K: Understand the various forms of transportation in your local environment.
S: Distinguish different forms of transportation and their benefits and limitations.
V: Appreciate the importance of exercising safety around transportation.

indexedPages.subject: Social Studies
essentialOutcomes: To understand that our celebrations help us build pride in our identity
specificOutcomes: K: State different forms of celebrations that are practiced in families to build pride and identity.
K: Identify aspects of culture such as food, music, dance that are relevant to celebrations in families.
S: Demonstrate different forms of celebration in the family and explain the importance of preserving local culture.
V: Appreciate their cultural heritage.
K: List religious and national festivals celebrated by the family.
S: Investigate and report how the family, school and nation celebrate various religious and national festivals.
V: Respect that families celebrate religious and national festivals in different ways, and that the families may choose not to celebrate some or all of these occasions.
K: Identify the national symbols of their country.
S: Describe the national symbols through drawing, painting and/or other expressions.
V: Appreciate that their national symbols are unique and special to their country and should be respected.

indexedPages.subject: Social Studies
essentialOutcomes: To understand how we shape the local environment and the local environment shapes who we are and how we live
specificOutcomes: K: Identify where your family lives.
S: Locate your home in relation to your closest neighbours.
V: Appreciate that your family lives in a neighbourhood.
K: Identify basic terms to describe relative location in your local environment such as near and far.
S: Distinguish between and show near and far in your immediate environment.
S: Use relative location to describe places in their neighbourhood.
V: Recognize the importance of being able to describe relative location for finding things and knowing your way around your immediate environment.

indexedPages.subject: Language Arts
essentialOutcomes: ELO 1: The learner will explore, use, and critically apply oral language for pleasure, personal growth, to form and foster relationships and to develop an appreciation and celebration of culture and of oral languages.
specificOutcomes: 1.1 Listen and respond appropriately to oral presentations, stories, and conversations
1.2 Use active listening skills: making eye contact, nodding, not interrupting
1.3 Ask and answer questions to seek clarification or additional information
1.4 Retell stories and events in sequence using beginning, middle, and end
1.5 Speak in complete sentences using Standard English and/or Home Language
1.6 Use appropriate volume, tone, and pace when speaking for different purposes
1.7 Participate in partner, small group, and whole class discussions
1.8 Share personal experiences and connect them to text or topics
1.9 Use descriptive language to express ideas and feelings clearly
1.10 Follow multi-step oral directions
1.11 Recite poems, songs, chants, and rhymes with expression
1.12 Demonstrate understanding of figurative language heard in texts
1.13 Begin to identify and use language appropriate to audience and purpose
1.14 Use Home Language and Standard English interchangeably as appropriate to context
1.15 Demonstrate listening behaviours that show respect for the speaker
indexedPages.summary: The learner will speak and listen: For pleasure, personal growth

eloGroups.elo: ELO 1: The learner will explore, use, and critically apply oral language for pleasure, personal growth, to form and foster relationships and to develop an appreciation and celebration of culture and of oral languages.

indexedPages.subject: Language Arts
essentialOutcomes: ELO 2: The learner will demonstrate a variety of ways to select and engage critically with a range of culturally diverse paper based, visual and digital texts for a variety of purposes, including pleasure, and personal growth.

ELO 3: The learner will demonstrate a variety of ways to use background knowledge and interests to select and engage critically with a range of culturally diverse paper based, visual, and digital texts for pleasure and personal growth.

ELO 4: Students will develop their understanding of how an author's purpose and style, genre, text form, text features and choice of vocabulary, and language influence the meaning of text and define the author's craft.
specificOutcomes: 2.1 Select texts for a variety of purposes using knowledge of text features (covers, titles, illustrations, headings)
2.2 Use prior knowledge and personal experience to make connections to text (text-to-self, text-to-text, text-to-world)
2.3 Make and confirm predictions using text features and prior knowledge
2.4 Identify and use basic reading strategies: rereading, reading ahead, sounding out
2.5 Demonstrate understanding of what is read through retelling and summarizing
2.6 Identify the main idea and supporting details in a text
2.7 Identify characters, setting, and plot in narrative texts
2.8 Distinguish between fiction and non-fiction texts
2.9 Use picture clues and context to determine the meaning of unfamiliar words
2.10 Respond personally to texts in a variety of ways (drawing, writing, talking)
2.11 Read and interpret simple visual texts (photographs, diagrams, maps, charts)
2.12 Engage with digital texts with teacher guidance
2.13 Read aloud with increasing fluency, accuracy, and expression
2.14 Identify and read a growing number of high-frequency words automatically
2.15 Apply phonics knowledge and word-solving strategies when reading
2.16 Demonstrate awareness of culturally diverse texts including texts from the Caribbean
3.1 Choose texts for independent reading based on personal interest
3.2 Respond to texts in a variety of ways: drawing, dramatizing, writing, discussing
3.3 Identify favourite authors, genres, and types of texts
3.4 Make connections between texts and personal experience, other texts, and the world
3.5 Discuss personal reactions and opinions about texts read
3.6 Identify and use features of a variety of text forms (stories, poems, labels, lists, charts)
3.7 Develop vocabulary through wide reading
3.8 Identify words with multiple meanings and begin to determine meaning from context

3.10 Share reading experiences with others (buddy reading, read aloud, book talks)
3.11 Develop awareness of how illustrations and visual features contribute to meaning
3.12 Begin to self-monitor reading for meaning and use fix-up strategies
4.1 Identify the author's purpose (to entertain, inform, persuade)
4.2 Identify and discuss text features: title, headings, table of contents, captions, glossary
4.3 Recognize different text forms and their purposes (stories, poems, letters, lists, reports)
4.4 Identify how word choice affects meaning and mood
4.5 Notice and discuss how an author uses descriptive language to paint pictures with words
4.6 Identify examples of rhyme, repetition, and rhythm in poetry
4.7 Compare how two texts on the same topic may differ in purpose, style, or perspective
4.8 Begin to identify figurative language (simile, alliteration) in text
4.9 Discuss how illustrations support or extend the meaning of text
4.10 Identify and discuss the genre of texts read (fairy tale, fable, non-fiction, poetry)
4.11 Use knowledge of genre features to support comprehension
4.12 Notice how authors use dialogue to develop characters

indexedPages.subject: Language Arts
essentialOutcomes: The learner will explore, use, and critically apply oral language for pleasure, personal growth, to foster relationships and to develop an appreciation and celebration of culture and of oral languages.
specificOutcomes: Listen and speak for pleasure and personal growth
2.1 Clarify opinions by responding to the questions and ideas/opinions of others through providing evidence to support thinking.
2.2 Continue to use intonation, tone, and expression to communicate ideas and feelings in a variety of situations, considering audience and purpose.
2.3 Discuss news, current events, opinions, feelings, strengths, challenges, future jobs and hopes in class discussions.
2.4 Reflect on and identify their strengths as listeners and speakers, areas for improvement and the strategies they found most helpful in oral communication situations.
2.5 Participate as active listeners in group learning activities by listening to: - identify the main points - determine the sequence events - give an accurate account - take notes

2.6 Report on a topic or text, tell a story, or recount an experience in an organized manner, using appropriate facts and relevant descriptive details to support main ideas or themes; speak clearly at an understandable pace.
2.7 Follow agreed-upon rules for discussions and carry out assigned roles.
2.8 Create and/or use visual aids in presentations when appropriate to enhance development of themes and/or main ideas (e.g., Graphics, sound)
2.9 Use appropriate facial expressions and gestures to support, accentuate, or dramatize the message
2.10 Adapt speech to a variety of contexts and tasks, using formal English when appropriate to task and situation.

2.11 Orally present an argument or perspective.12 Create, presents, participates in mini debates/speeches using persuasive techniques e.g., promises, dares, flattery, glittering generalities, intonations that persuades people to buy or do something.
2.12 Identify the reasons and evidence a speaker provides to support points.
2.13 Respond to and to give sequential multi-step directions and instructions with increasing detail
2.14 Listen to identify and discuss critically persuasive techniques used in advertisements
Listen and speak to form and foster relationships

indexedPages.subject: Language Arts
essentialOutcomes: ELO 1.1: The learner will explore, use, and critically apply oral language for pleasure, personal growth, to foster relationships and to develop an appreciation and celebration of culture and of oral languages.
specificOutcomes: 1.1 Listen to music, conversation and environmental sounds for personal enjoyment
1.2 Demonstrate interest, curiosity, engagement in sharing the experiences of others and with oral stories and information sharing
1.3 Use social listening and speaking skills to interact with a variety of audiences with sensitivity and respect
1.4 Interact and collaborate with the teacher and children who have diverse interests, backgrounds and languages
1.5 Become aware of how effective listening enhances understanding
1.6 Observe how tone, fluency and intonation impact meaning and mood
1.7 Use Home Language(s) and, as Standard English develops, share their thoughts, feelings and questions with increasing confidence
1.8 Develop increasing clarity and focus when sharing stories or experiences
1.9 Engage in active phonological awareness activities and word play to discriminate between various sounds
1.10 Develop and apply vocabulary and language structures to enhance their understanding of how to communicate ideas with purpose and focus

ELO 2.3: Learners will develop their understanding of how an author's choice of vocabulary, language, genre, text form, text features and style influence the meaning of text.
